text:
One of the greatest survival horror games ever made!

Even after all these years, Resident Evil HD Remaster feels incredibly polished and well designed. The atmosphere is unmatched, the Spencer Mansion is a masterpiece of level design, and every room keeps you on edge.

Unlike many modern games that hold your hand, this game trusts the player. Resource management matters, exploration is rewarding, and solving puzzles feels genuinely satisfying. Every shortcut unlocked and every key item discovered feels like real progress...(lol it was a nightmare yet fun)

The pre-rendered backgrounds still look beautiful, the soundtrack creates constant tension, and the Crimson Heads add an extra layer of fear even for returning players and on top of that hunters ugh....

I went for the Platinum achievement, and every playthrough showed me something new. It never stopped being fun! I loved how it all started, the journey of Chris and Jill as the early main cast of RE, it was a wonderful experience.

If you're interested in survival horror, this isn't just a game you should play, it's a game you should experience.

A true masterpiece and one of Capcom's finest game!
label: analysis

text:
Holds up really well. The Spencer Mansion is an iconic location for reason, and it's the best part of this game. The game paces itself expertly with its location, becoming more sinister as you uncover more of its secrets. The lore itself is also very strong. The game succeeds in making a tense experience by limiting resources, obfuscating vision with the static camera, and limiting saves. While it did annoy me at first, I came to appreciate how slow and deliberate the gunplay is because it amplifies the threat of all enemies, especially fast ones. The QoL in this game is good too.

I had an issue with the dog enemies because their speed and small stature made them aggravating to fight. Since you can only aim vertically in 3 set angles (up, neutral, down), it means I had to wait for them to get close before I could actually target them. This led to otherwise avoidable damage; you can't move whilst aiming either. Furthermore, I did find the backtracking a bit tedious and excessive and the bossfights dull. 

Personally, I don't like limited saves. I get its use, and it did successfully increase the tension; however, practically speaking, I find it hinders my experience more than helps it. I should clarify, I'm not saying this is objectively bad, just giving a word of warning for anyone else who also doesn't like this mechanic. 

Nevertheless, this is a must play for any [i]Resident Evil[/i] fan, and I strongly recommend it to anyone that likes horror games.
label: analysis

text:
This game is extremely rough to play as someone who never played it growing up, it feels almost like a prototype test demo for RE2, the (in my opinion) far superior game. 

I'm fairly convinced the only praise this game gets is from people wearing heavy nostalgia goggles, because personally I don't think this one stands the test of time in the slightest.

The atmosphere is good and the art direction for the game is very solid, voice actors aren't the best but at least the lines are funny. Everything else? Absolutely awful.

It's not even the tank controls or the horrid camera angles either, those I can actually put up with just fine.

The game isn't actually scary at all, its more "cheesy" if anything, which is the opposite of what most people seem to claim, not only that but the entire game is just trial and error moon logic puzzles while giving you super limited inventory slots where you often (not always) gotta randomly guess what items are gonna be useful for the immediate future or not. 

Perfect example of this games issues in a nutshell, the shield crest at the start of the game. It doesn't come into play until HOURS later. Just...why?

So you might just end up carrying it around hoping it'll be useful soon because "Why would they give us a solution with no problem?"

This game is riddled with poor game design choices and "puzzles" that fluctuate between fine and awful design. They aren't even hard either, they're just...bad.

The entire game is just extremely tedious and boring. Even the load screens waste your time which I had to go grab a mod to fix so I wouldn't lose my mind listening to that stupid door sound given how much backtracking this game requires.

The enemy design is also made to be extremely boring and a waste of your time, especially the boss fights, which to be fair the bosses in the other RE games could be this way too, but its especially bad here where its just a boring bullet sponge that takes what feels like 10 years to die.

RE2 is a far better experience and I highly recommend just skipping this one and going straight into RE2, it's got 1000x better game design principles.

I give it a 2/10.
label: analysis

text:
This game is Awesome but in a hard way, what i mean by that this game is stupidly confusing in the start but once you get the hang of it becomes relatively easy and start understanding what do you have to do in this game.
My first playthrough being with jill (normal difficulty) was hard i would say as i didn’t have much knowledge what did i had to do so i died plenty of times and somehow ran out of ink ribbons as well, making my playthrough Hella rough but still i managed to beat the game in 11 hrs on normal.
Moving on to my Chris's playthrough (Hard difficulty), man, it was a completely different scenario. Having only six inventory slots instead of eight is no joke, especially when you have to carry around old keys since he doesn't get a lockpick. It forces you to be way more strategic with your item management and when you actually decide to use your fuel to burn a zombie even though the enemies hit harder, the run actually felt smoother overall because I already knew the mansion layout from my first time through.
Overall, once you get past that initial brutal learning curve, the game is an absolute masterpiece. If you love classic survival horror and don't mind a genuine challenge, you have to play it.
label: analysis

text:
Resident Evil HD Remastered — 10/10

Beautiful survival horror.

At first: fear, confusion, panic.

Later: routing, precision, control over your panic.

The mansion doesn’t change its manners, you do bud. 

Every mistake hurts. Every correct decision feels extremely deserved.

Save rooms are still perfect. Calm in a place that wants you completely dead.

Inventory management is the real enemy.

Hunters still remind you that your neck ain’t safe. 

Crimson Heads still punish dumb mistakes.

And yet… you come back. Why?
Game has great replay ability. 
At the time of writing this reviews, I had just finished my 10th, full Platinum/100% run. 

Oh!
One last thing.
Bring a healing item to boss fights and keep your inventory mostly empty!
Hope you enjoy the game.

text:
This game's opening first hours in the police station are some of the most dreadful I have ever encountered. I remember my father and older brother playing this game on our PS1 back in 98/99. The new RE Engine makes what was scary then nearly unbearable today (in a good way).

I felt completely helpless in the first areas, or at least until Leon got his handy dandy shotgun. Claire's guns were always lackluster at first, but once you get the hang of dodging and running around zombies, figuring out the large number of puzzles, and getting lost in the labyrinth of hallways, tunnels, and mazes, you will learn to love this game. I am happy this got a remake. The third person camera makes this game so much better, let alone the modern graphics and true darkness of the RPD hallways.

Fantastic job Capcom's RE2 Remake team. You made me nearly pee myself in a game where I know nothing can physically hurt me, but psychologically, I was made to suffer very real duress. Thank you for the well-crafted upgrade to an all-time great game.
label: analysis

text:
Overall Score - 8/10

Resident Evil 2 Remake is the game that made me fall in love with the Resident Evil franchise as a whole. It's captivating and does an AMAZING service to the original, which I also played (and honestly enjoyed much more than the remake, but that's a review for another day). The puzzles, the absolute terror I felt whenever I saw Mr. X, the environment! There are a lot of aspects to this game that I could rant about forever and ever, but what really gets me is the simplicity of it. You've got zombies and two campaigns where you're playing as relatively normal people who just want to help and escape with their lives. Leon and Claire are some of the best RE protagonists we have. I think that the intertwining of Leon and Claire's stories could have been done a bit more intricately, but that's really a minor complaint when it comes to RE2R. Both Leon A and Claire B are incredibly done, and it's so cool to see the level of detail the devs put into making the runs unique yet similar to one another. Seeing the same location from a different perspective really changes your experience.

TLDR; If you're new to Resident Evil, start with this game. If you're anything like me, you'll end up seventeen games later writing a nine paragraph steam review.

text:
[h2]RE2 Remake: A masterclass in survival horror[/h2]


After playing Resident Evil 1 a few months ago, I was honestly a little concerned about how much I would enjoy Resident Evil 2. While I could appreciate what RE1 was trying to do, a lot of its older design choices made it difficult for me to fully enjoy in 2026. Thankfully, RE2 Remake takes everything that made the classic Resident Evil games great and modernises it without losing what made them special in the first place.

After completing both Leon and Claire's campaigns, I can safely say I understand why this game is held in such high regard. It delivers fantastic survival horror gameplay, an incredible atmosphere, memorable characters, and some genuinely terrifying moments that had me constantly on edge.


[h3]The Good[/h3]


The first thing that stood out to me was the atmosphere. From the moment you step into the Raccoon City Police Department, the game does an incredible job of making you feel uncomfortable. Every dark corridor feels dangerous, every door could hide something waiting to jump out at you, and the constant lack of resources means you're always thinking carefully about every encounter. The Police Station itself is brilliantly designed and was easily my favourite area in the game. Slowly unlocking new sections, finding shortcuts, solving puzzles and learning the layout was incredibly satisfying.

The gameplay is where RE2 Remake really shines. It manages to keep the survival horror identity of the older games while making everything feel modern and smooth to play. Resource management remains important, enemies stay threatening for most of the game, and exploration is constantly rewarded. In fact, I somehow managed to miss the shotgun and several other weapons for almost my entire first Leon playthrough, meaning I got to the end of the sewers with little more than my starting pistol. While that was entirely my own fault, it showed just how much the game rewards players who take the time to properly explore.

One of the biggest highlights of the game is The Tyrant, known unofficially as Mr. X. I genuinely think he is one of the best horror enemies I've encountered in gaming. Hearing his footsteps getting closer while you're trying to solve a puzzle or search a room creates an incredible amount of tension. The first time he smashed through a wall in front of me nearly made me jump out of my chair. Even after completing both campaigns, he never really stopped being intimidating. The fact that he remains a constant threat without becoming overly frustrating is a huge achievement.

The presentation is also fantastic. The graphics still look incredible, with highly detailed character models, excellent lighting, and some genuinely impressive environmental detail. Combined with the excellent sound design, the game does a brilliant job of keeping the tension high. The music that plays when Mr. X spots you immediately puts you into panic mode, while the audio during the plant zombie sections was some of the most unsettling sound design I've heard in a horror game.

I also really enjoyed both campaigns. Leon and Claire are both likeable protagonists and I never found myself wishing I was playing as one over the other. Leon's campaign was probably my favourite from a gameplay perspective because I preferred his weapons, while Claire's campaign had some stronger story moments, particularly her relationship with Sherry. The differences between the A and B scenarios were also larger than I expected, giving the game a lot of replay value and making a second playthrough feel worthwhile rather than repetitive.


[h3]The Bad[/h3]


The biggest criticism I have is that while the story is good, it never reaches the level of greatness that some of gaming's best narratives achieve. There are some strong character moments and a few memorable twists, but this is very much a game carried by its gameplay, atmosphere and level design rather than its story.

I also think the A and B scenarios were a slight missed opportunity. I enjoyed both campaigns and appreciated the differences between them, but I would have loved to see them more directly interconnected. Instead, they often feel like alternate versions of events rather than two stories happening alongside one another. It isn't a major issue, but it feels like there was potential for something even better.

Finally, while the Sewers and Laboratory are both good areas, neither of them quite reaches the same level of quality as the Police Station. The opening section of the game is so strong that the later environments struggle to match it.


[h3]Final Thoughts[/h3]


Resident Evil 2 Remake is exactly what a remake should be. It successfully modernises a classic game while preserving everything that made the original special. The gameplay is excellent, the atmosphere is fantastic, the presentation still holds up incredibly well, and Mr. X is one of the most memorable horror antagonists I've encountered in a game.

More importantly, this is the Resident Evil game I would recommend to newcomers. While I can appreciate the importance of Resident Evil 1, Resident Evil 2 Remake feels like the perfect entry point for modern players. It delivers the survival horror experience the series is known for without many of the frustrations that come with older game design.

It may not have a masterpiece-level story, but almost everything else it does is outstanding.


[h2]Rating: 9.0/10[/h2]

text:
Part of a series of remakes by Capcom, this remake came after RE7, which personally was my introduction to the franchise. As a child, I loved watching horror but not playing them! I haven't actually finished watching RE2R when I first started playing this game, and I was in for a ride. RE2R was technically the first RE game I have tried, but isn't the first RE game I completed, as that title belongs to RE4R. Playing through the early sections of the game, I got so scared, I stopped for 1-2 years, before mustering up my courage to beat RE4R and then this game.

I've heard horror stories of people softlocking themselves, but as a perfectionist, I tried to get every item in my first run as Leon and luckily didn't face that problem. The first time anxiety crept in was when I encountered Mr X unknowingly. He kept appearing and chasing after me half the time and that was pure panic-inducing. I believe the highlight of this game is definitely the run-ins with Mr X... Playing through and beating this game has definitely given me some more confidence in playing horror games.

The more times you interact and/or die to an enemy, makes you less scared of the game! And watching it versus playing and experiencing it is COMPLETELY different, and sure did not make me less scared for the Regeneradors in RE4R or for Mr X...

text:
For $60, the amount of insane quality and polish you get here is staggering. If you like games that actually make you uncomfortable instead of just holding your hand, this is the absolute peak of the genre. Capcom took notes on everything that worked in RE7 (crafting, the weapon wheel, diegetic cassette tapes) and fixed everything that didn't.

THE CAMPAIGN STRUCTURE:
Play Leon A, and then play Claire B if you want a true challenge. It is a perfect pairing, and for the first time, the A and B scenarios feel like narrative equals. Leon is no longer a passive spectator, and Claire’s fierce, protective characterization is easily the best version of her in the franchise.

THE CLAIRE B ARSENAL:
While Leon gets standard heavy-duty firearms, Claire gets a completely unique, highly tactical loadout:

Handgun: Utilizes enhanced ammo making a brilliant return from RE7.

Grenade Launcher: Forces tactical choices between Flame and Acid rounds depending on the enemy.

Spark Shot: A unique slot-waster that deals massive damage, provided you are patient and good with your spatial positioning.

Submachine Gun: Brilliantly rebalanced from 1998—buffed because you can craft the ammo, but nerfed with a 24-round magazine and lower damage. Incredibly useful for the final G5 fight.

Minigun: The ultimate, earned payoff for the G4 fight.

THE SURVIVAL ECONOMY CLIMAX:
That sounds like a lot of firepower, but ammo is brutally scarce—you either get ammo you don't needor you craft the ammo you want yourself. I found myself completely out of ammo and heals against the G4 and G5 Birkin fights on the B Scenario. That is exactly how Survival Horror is meant to be played: you should be down on one knee, completely desperate, shooting a boss to pieces with whatever bullets you have left. This game delivers that exact tension perfectly.

THE MECHANICS & AI:

No Free Defense: Unlike RE7, there is no zero-cost block button to cheese encounters. You actually have to respect the physical space of the hallways. There are defensive items which make a return from RE1 Remake. 

The Apex Predator: Mr. X is a calculated, terrifying force of nature who chases everybody regardless of your scenario. In Leon's story he is an active antagonist. His dynamic stalking forces you to make reckless errors and ruins your perfectly planned save-room trips.

The Minor Characters: Kendo, Marvin, and Annette Birkin are completely fleshed out with devastating emotional weight. Even Ben Bertolucci is vastly improved; his pure, expressive terror perfectly establishes the stakes.

THE CUT CONTENT:
Yes, the 1998 "Zapping System" is gone, but honestly, it makes total sense. Trying to code that level of persistent state-tracking into the massive, dynamic physics ecosystem of the RE Engine would be a nightmare. The sheer quality of the standalone campaigns more than makes up for it.

text:
I have mixed feelings about this one. Yes, it is short. Yes, some things don't make sense. Yes, it feels like someone started to piece a puzzle together but used wrong pieces on certain parts. I did, however, not hate it. Did I like it? Hmm, I think it was alright. They could have done so much more with this one. I would have loved to see that! The game itself looks amazing, the game mechanics were also fun, and the dynamic between Jill and Carlos? Loved it. Jill is a power house of a woman, and Carlos got heart and charm, so I'm sad they didn't do more with it!

So, for this, we will do some pros and cons, starting with the pros:

-Setup was interesting, seeing a different perspective of the same night/events of when things got to ♥♥♥♥ in Racoon City
-Environment looked beautiful
-Dynamic between Jill and Carlos was fun
-Carlos little jokes and interaction with the environment
-Nemesis was a badass of a main enemy
-Save-room music was amazing, overall sound design was great
-Game was the grossest yet so far
-Hospital area was really fun

Here are the cons:

-I felt that there weren't enough puzzle elements
-Story sometimes felt thrown together with scenes clearly missing
-Clocktower, what even was that?
-Not enough scare moments
-Would have been nice to see Carlos during the last part for Jill more, like what was he doing?!

Saying this, I believe that I enjoyed the game probably way more thanks to playing it together on stream with my community, as we always come up with some fun stuff. I don't think I would have enjoyed it as much if I would have just played it by myself. I do still recommend playing this, as overall it was fun.

Here's the rating:

Horror                       6/10
Action                       8/10
Puzzle                       5/10
Story                         5/10
Cheese Dialogue   8/10 (Carlos saved that one)

Overall:                     6/10

text:
RESIDENT EVIL 3 CAN BE GREAT TO WORST DEPENDING ON YOUR EXPECTATIONS FROM THE GAME.
Resident evil 3 for me is decent mainline title or for me it felt like a great expansion to the racoon city series. My first game in this series is Resident evil 2 which is an absolute banger of a game. I further played Resident evil 7 which has a completely different story which later connected to the main timeline, The main reason I continued with 7 because it was a completely different setting story but the roots are the survival horror of resident evil 2. Later  I played Resident evil 4 which i found to be good but it was completely action. I left 3 for the last because it was the one of the hated titles of the mainline series after Resident evil 6. I paid the least for Resident evil 3 compared any other mainline title. I went into the game just to get some more fun in the raccoon city setting and the events in the game are connected to Resident evil 2 which was enough for me to play it.  After completing it I enjoyed my time but I also felt how the game just felt like a filler or more like a spin off rather than its own thing. Original resident evil 3 was nothing like this it was its own thing it further explored the game mechanics and tried several new ones and was better than original resident evil 2 in every manner. But the RE3 remake just waters down every mechanic of RE2 remake making it feel like more like a DLC. The game has a lot lot more potential than what it is but sadly not utilized. Still the game is fun but short the map is nice to explore but its simple and has nothing worthy to revisit or backtrack. The puzzles are very less and very bad . Enemy types are fresh and Boss battles are good and feels new compared to all the mainline titles in the franchise. The horror is less action is more but it is good. The game has many problems fundamentally and logically but its still worth the experience. The performance is good but the optimization is bad there are a lot stutters, freezes although the reasons for this simple but its annoying. You have remove all overlay applications and lock your fps to get smooth fps with some rare stutters but still its a disappointment considering that this the only game in the franchise that i faced this problem with and has no clear solution or bug fix even after all these years. The game looks amazing the gameplay is fun, story just expands the lore in a different perspective. When to play the game completely depends on your expectations and price your gonna pay.
RESIDENT EVIL 3 IS A SHORT BUT FUN RIDE WITH MANY FLAWS AND IMPORTANTLY IT FEELS MORE LIKE AN EXPANSION TO RE2 WHICH IS DISAPPOINTING WHEN COMPARED TO THE ORIGINAL RE3 
TOTAL-7/10

text:
Do not let the title fool you: Resident Evil 3 (2020) pivots hard away from the Metroidvania puzzle-box design of the RE2 Remake. It trades branching paths and exploration for a highly linear, corridor-heavy experience that feels much closer to the action-packed pacing of Resident Evil 4. It is one of the harder games in the modern engine, but it comes with a lot of structural compromises.

THE GOOD (Characters & Combat Tension):

The Protagonists: The characterization here is fantastic. Jill and Carlos are incredibly well-written. The expanded roles for minor characters like Tyrell and Mikhail are a massive highlight—Mikhail in particular is handled brilliantly, portrayed as a man who respects Jill and genuinely cares about the civilian collateral damage.

Nemesis: The boss fights against Nemesis are consistently intense. Whether it is a scripted set-piece or a dynamic encounter, he demands absolute respect and keeps the adrenaline high.

Zombie Sponginess: Surprisingly, standard zombies have significantly more HP this time around. This actually enhances the survival horror aspect slightly compared to RE2R, forcing you to really commit to taking a target down or burning resources.

THE MIXED (Mechanics & Combat Loop):

The Knife Economy: The knife was tweaked from RE2R. It is now infinite (a massive buff), but it takes up a permanent inventory slot and does less damage. I found myself using it to down enemies constantly, which ironically made me ignore the new dodge mechanic.

The Dodge: The dodge mechanic is back and incredibly responsive. It works exactly as intended, even if you can largely get by just using knife-downs.

Lazy Defense: The brilliant sub-weapon defensive items (shoving grenades/knives into grab attacks) from RE2R are gone. They were replaced by button-mashing QTEs, which feels like a massive, lazy step backward for the combat mechanics.

THE UGLY (Cut Content & Puzzles):

The Chopping Block: The biggest sin of this remake is the cut content. The iconic Clock Tower is reduced to a background skybox, the Gravedigger boss is completely missing, and the game ends in another NEST facility, which feels highly repetitive.

Nikolai's Rewrite: The voice performance for Nikolai is great, but his narrative rewrite is weak. Changing his motivation from pure corporate greed to being a vague "grunt for a rival company" that is never fully explained robs him of his agency as a villain.

Zero Brain Power: The original RE3 had some genuinely challenging puzzles. This remake has exactly one. It completely abandons the puzzle-solving core of the franchise.

VERDICT:
It is a fine, high-octane game, but it is incredibly short and suffers from annoying cut content. Structurally and chronologically, this feels like it should have been packaged as a $30 standalone DLC for the RE2 Remake rather than a full-priced release. Catch it on a deep sale.

text:
Overall score - 6/10

SPOILERS BELOW!

The black sheep of the remakes. However, don't let that foreboding title turn you away from Resident Evil 3 Remake. This was the second Resident Evil game I had ever played (coming from RE2R) and I had a ton of fun on my first playthrough, probably the most fun I've had while playing a Resident Evil game. Nemesis was terrifying during his encounters. I really enjoyed playing as Jill and Carlos. Running around Raccoon City and figuring out how to progress was tedious, but it paid off. Of course, now that I've played RE3 OG and seen what we could have had... I must say that it's a huge let down. RE3R is an insanely short game compared to its sister game, RE2R, and even it's original. It cut recognizable locations like the Clocktower and the Park, and it really lacks a lot of substance. One of my least favorite aspects of RE3R is the fact that they try so hard to push Nikolai as the "real" bad guy opposed to Nemesis, the bio-engineered super soldier that was sent to kill you. The handling of Nemesis overall was poor. The game immediately strips him of any inkling that he is highly intelligent by turning him into a dog beast, and then a literal mush monster by the end of the game. RE3R had a lot of potential, and unfortunately I don't think we'll see a proper remake of RE3 for a long time.

Maybe my toxic trait is that I like this a good chunk more than RE2 remake, even with all of its cut content. That should probably be a testament more so to the original but admittedly I've not gotten around to that yet.

text:
Resident Evil 4 remake is a masterclass in modernizing a classic, successfully preserving the identity of the original 2005 masterpiece while updating it for contemporary gaming standards. Mechanically, the game introduces fluent movement while aiming, a dynamic parrying system with Leon’s knife, and redesigned combat areas that encourage greater tactical freedom. These enhancements make the action feel incredibly fluid and intense, elevating the tension without sacrificing the survival horror roots. The developers also expanded the level design, deepening the puzzles and exploration elements to create a seamless world that feels both hauntingly familiar and excitingly unpredictable.

Visually and narratively, the remake elevates the experience into a gripping, atmospheric thriller. Powered by the RE Engine, the game features stunningly detailed environments, realistic lighting, and grotesque enemy designs that enhance the overall sense of dread. The story receives a meaningful upgrade as well, grounding the narrative with deeper character development, more realistic dialogue, and expanded roles for supporting characters like Ashley Graham and Luis Serra. By flawlessly blending modernized gameplay mechanics with a darker, more cinematic tone, the Resident Evil 4 remake honors its legacy while standing on its own as an exceptional, high-stakes action-horror experience.

text:
Resident Evil 4 is one of those games that is held in super high regard and for good reason. However, I do believe that you really can’t understand how fun the game really is until you have played it, especially coming off of RE2. This game takes a lot about what you know about Resident Evil games thus far, keeps some of it and flips other parts on its head. No longer are you the novice, scared or underprepared main character of a Resident Evil game who is truly surviving the experience they are unfortunately held in, rather the natural progression of a returning character who had undergone some training to return as the danger itself. Throughout this whole game, even on initial playthroughs, you get a similar feeling you would get in a game like RE1 or RE2 replaying with infinite ammo, that power over that which had sought out to kill you is there from the get-go even with limited things on your initial or new saves. Leon is now a mature and snappy adult who keeps many of his quips, deeply damaged from the events of the Raccoon City outbreak he is now ready to really become something to behold. The gameplay is new in that sense, I am not cynical enough to call RE4 no longer a survival horror, but the game really feels like its an Action than a horror than a survival game as you really aren’t in any circumstance where you feel that dread like in the first or second games. With that being said though that is not a bad thing, they actually handled that quite well and like I mentioned felt like the natural progression of not necessarily Resident Evil, but specifically Resident Evil when an old character had returned to the big screen, who had underwent their time being afraid and in danger and now is allowed to evolve into something cooler. 
The story of this game too is completely out of our are of expertise with the Arklay mountains and Raccoon City, you find yourself in Spain working for the government to retrieve the president’s daughter from an infected bio terror cult who are seeking to sacrifice her to their god. We do find out later how that actually does tie into the events of Raccoon City but I will leave that out of the review for good faith. I need to go through the original still but this game looks fantastic and the soundtrack is great as usual too, I am sure it was even cooler for long time fans who had played RE4 original and held it in such high regard already. The gameplay does feel great, very responsive gunplay and the melee’s are so satisfying be it the kicks, suplex or whatever leon really feels like a super soldier on a mission. Even the DLC was honestly quite enjoyable, Ada’s gameplay is much better than any of her segments in RE2 and is just as enjoyable to play as Leon in my opinion, and the DLC itself was a neat recounting of events from Ada’s perspective during Leon’s campaign which was quite cool.
One does have to mention though the unfortunate performance of Ada’s “new” voice actress in this game, really took me out of some scenes especially in the base campaign when everyone is consistently amazing, I hope she gets a better place to do more work in studio and a bit more practice, there were even a few moments during Seperate ways where I didn’t think she was terrible but overall the performance is quite weak especially compared to RE2R Ada. Overall though this game is fantastic, one of my favourites games for sure, the difficulties all felt good, rewards are cool, mercenaries is a treat for this game truthfully, gameplay, sound, and story all great there is not much bad to say about it in all honesty and I totally understand why this is considered one of the best games of all time and in discussion for THE best game.
label: analysis

RE4 Remake is a lot of fun and stays true to the original, but I had some performance issues on Steam Deck. 

The game would slow down whenever I resumed from standby, which was frustrating. After some trial and error, I found that the fix was simple: I kept about half my storage free on both the SSD and SD card, and did a full restart of my Steam Deck. After that, everything ran smoothly.

Now the game plays really well with good graphics. It's a solid experience as long as you have enough free storage space.

 Definitely worth playing if you're willing to manage your storage like I did.

text:
TLDR - Decent story and a good setting but let down by clunky movement and initially tedious combat. 

The story and setting is quite good - mysterious village filled with cultists etc  - and the ambience is great. They do tend to reuse the map a lot so there's a lot of running backwards and forwards over ground you've already covered but it felt like they realised they were pushing it and moved you on to new bits of the map every now and then. There are also a lot of treasures to find which require backtracking, though there's enough upgrades to give you an incentive to do it.

Unfortunately, the running backwards and forwards tends to be more tedious than it should be because your character moves like a drunken, arthritic grandmother who's wading through molasses. It's a seriously sluggish, unresponsive game. He's supposedly some sort of top secret agent and looks like he's in his twenties but I know seventy year olds who move faster than this guy does. Hell, the little old lady cultists you encounter would run rings around him. 

Combat is a bit of a mixed bag - there's a lot of it because enemies constantly respawn indeed, often just materialise behind you. You can sneak a bit, run past some but it fells like you're meant to fight most stuff. I didn't like it to start - enemies are bullet sponges and I felt like I was firing a pea shooter - but once I'd gotten some better weapons and upgraded them it was a lot more enjoyable and felt much more fluid.

Overall, I thought it was.... alright. Not awful - I finished it and I enjoyed most of it - but not superb or anything. I get that twenty years ago this was new and shiny but, playing it in 2026, it felt quite dated and unnecessarily clunky. Worth a look on sale, but I'd definitely wait for one.

text:
Resident Evil 4 Remake is a game I enjoyed, but it's also one that became less interesting the longer it went on. The presentation is fantastic, with great visuals, strong atmosphere, and some genuinely memorable locations. The opening hours, especially in the village, are excellent and show why this game is considered a classic.

The combat feels satisfying at first. Weapons have weight, enemies react well to damage, and upgrading your arsenal through the Merchant is always rewarding. The problem is that the game doesn't evolve enough over time. After a certain point, you're doing the same things over and over again: entering an area, fighting another group of enemies, collecting loot, moving to the next area, and repeating the process. What starts as exciting eventually becomes predictable.

A big reason for this is the enemy variety. It often feels like the game keeps throwing the same enemies at you in different locations. There are new enemy types throughout the campaign, but not enough to stop encounters from feeling repetitive. The loot system has a similar issue. Nearly every corner contains the same resources, money, or treasure, making exploration feel more routine than rewarding.

The story is easily one of the weakest aspects of the game. Honestly, it almost feels like there isn't much of a story at all. Leon is sent on a mission, things happen, bosses appear, and then the game ends. The plot mostly exists to push the player toward the next combat encounter. While the characters are entertaining, I never felt invested in the narrative itself.


The Shooting Range was probably my favorite side activity in the game. Whenever I found one, I always stopped to do all the challenges. It was a nice change from the usual gameplay and gave me a break from fighting the same enemies over and over. Trying to get better scores and unlock charms was actually really fun, and I ended up spending more time there than I expected.


Despite these issues, I still think Resident Evil 4 Remake is a really good game. The gameplay is polished, the presentation is excellent, and the first two-thirds of the adventure are consistently enjoyable. I just wish there was more enemy variety, a stronger story, and a better final act to keep things fresh until the credits rolled.
label: analysis

text:
This is the the most arcade feeling Resident Evil in the mainline series, heavily focused on co-op mechanics while ditching puzzles and exploration. While those co-op mechanics are implemented incredibly well, the solo experience can be a grueling pain. The boss fights in particular are designed with co-op in mind, which become unnecessarily frustrating and tedious when you have to depend on an AI companion. 

That said, the game is a pretty well paced 9ish hour experience with a good variety of locations and encounters. The Africa setting and history behind the progenitor virus is great. The combat flow is similar to RE4, mixing in gunplay and melee strikes. Though at times the game becomes a cover shooter, and a few chapters become on-rail shooters. And RE5's mercenary is filled with a ton of stages and characters to play as.

text:
What a sweet Resident Evil Coop Gem.

+ Great RE game
+ Co-Op experience
+ Amazing and simple inventory management
+ Hidden unlockable bonuses, like best weapons

o Aim system is unique but you can get used to it
o Needs a little tinkering to work flawlessly on Steam Deck

- No negatives coming to my mind

For Steam Deckers to get rid of freezes:
1) Properties of game -> Compatibility, check the box and select the newest "Proton GE"
2) Paste this into Launch options: PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1 PROTON_NO_FSYNC=1 %command%
3) Launch game, press Deck's button (...), select Battery, turn on "Use per-game profile" and set 60FPS (60Hz)
4) In game settings, make sure to have V-Sync on and reduce FPS to 60
5) You can restart the game, sometimes it launches on second try after changing all these settings. Enjoy!

text:
Game has old movement mechanics, if your used to tank controls then you may enjoy it, Found it fustrating to move quickly away from enemies at times even towards the end of the game. When your aiming down sight, reloading or using an item you can't move, which can make it difficult in the smaller places that you will be fighting in often. 
Most enemies are almost the same to the prior RE games with slight differences which isn't a bad thing, won't recommend the game as there feels like there are too many enemies that can just one shot and feel more like bullet sponges to make you really think about the resources you use on them and some of them feel more difficult then the bosses.
Speaking of bosses, they are alright but some just feel like they were quickly made to fill in time, There are moments in this game where i feels like fun but honestly it can be far and few between and it feels like i'm forcing myself just to finish the game. 
all in all, it's an alright game, nothing to actively go out of your way for, I just feel there is more to not like in this game then there is to like in the game.

text:
Yes, despite its flaws, it's still fun.

I played with a community patch that restored a bunch of things this PC port was missing. Even with those enhancements, we encountered some absurd bugs, such as the Uroboros Mkono boss being invincible, forcing us to buy a rocket launcher to progress through the game. My inventory also became invisible and uninteractable during the Item Management screen around 6-7 times.

That said, it clearly carries elements from RE4 but tries to give them a more cinematic, QTE-heavy, and shooter-focused approach. I didn't mind it, but the last 3 chapters didn't feel great, considering they have enemies with heavy armor, holdout sequences, and certain annoying puzzles.

I played the whole thing with a friend in co-op. We loved spamming the X and V keys for callouts. Overall, it was a decent Resident Evil game. I simply wish the PC experience was smoother.
label: analysis

text:
Story & Characters:
It's an RE game. Both pretty bullshitty, stupid and fun. Just about what you'd expect really, no weird surprises in any good or bad direction. Cheesy dialogue & bad voice acting left and right, with a very funnily overdone story. 6/10

Character Movement & Gameplay Mechanics:
Hasn't aged well to sum it up. I just played the game for the first time and the controls are frustrating at best. I unironically feel like the original Ocarina of Time controlled smoother than this game. But then again: I feel like it really wasn't made for mouse and keyboard at all. I tried a section of the game with controller and it did feel much better!

It feels a bit wasted to upgrade any weapon at all when 2 chapters later you just find a better version of the same weapon or you can just buy a better one. I'm sure that System works much better in ng+, considering most RE games are built for being beat multiple times, but for a single playthrough the weapon system really felt anythign but rewarding. I do like the partner mechanic however. Despite how clunky it actually may be, it often feels oddly fluid. I experienced the entire game in singleplayer, but I'm sure this game is lots of fun when playing as 2. But even here there are many issues. The partner often doesn't move very intuitively. In a bossfight in chapter 6.2 for example Sheva for some reason doesn't move AT ALL unless being told to and even then she will return right back to her spot where she's stuck at. 3.5/10

Combat & Encounter design:
There's many places in this game, where I was very impressed with how combat was designed and euqally as many if not more places, where I was dumbfounded at how not thought through it seemed. Bosses go from "very creative" to "hold you gun into their face for 5 minutes". Regular encounters go from, "shoot two guys" to "This is like 40 enemies. Good luck figuring out which of them drop amo". I would average the score at a 5 here, but the creativity in many places really does save it for me. 6/10

Environmental design:
While not very pretty, the game has tons of diversity when it comes to design, especially considering the game is mostly one singular setting. The environment actually feels well buitlt for and around the game. Many places look and are very "video gamie", but that's just about the only complaint I have. Pretty good actually, but nothing too special. 8/10

Music & Audio:
The music is decent, but nothing special and most weapons don't really feel as impactful when shooting them as they should in my opinion. What I like most is all the sounds the UI makes, but I feel like in most other places the audio could have been better. It's not bad enough to negatively impact the experiece however and the music is still at the very least good in the places where there is music. So still a solid 6/10

Pacing:
This might be a me-issue, but I do NOT feel like I've only played this game for 11 hours. There where many places in the game where I entered a loading zone and just HOPED that the chapter I'm in was finally over.  I don't even feel like the game did have bad pacing however. I do still feel like many chapters could have had a couple rooms less at the very least. Many encounters did drag the game a lot. I do have to complimnt the checkpoint system however. They always do feel very well places. 5.5/10

Over all I feel like the age of the game is holding back many of it's aspects, but despite me really not enjoying MANY parts of the game, I would never say it's a bad game. The characters and world around them are very charming and the ideas just work most of the time.

Many many action sequences are downright impressive still, even by todays standards and like any other RE game the replay value is (probably) insane. If tis game ever gets a remake, it will probably be very good. Like this, it's just decent I feel. But likable. 

Total 5.8/10
label: analysis

text: Resident Evil 5 is the ultimate boulder-punching simulator. If you have ever looked at a solid, multi-ton volcanic rock and thought, "I want to uppercut that," then this is the game for you. While the horror elements of the older games are gone, it completely makes up for it with peak action, hilarious co-op chaos, and Chris Redfield's absurdly massive biceps. The inventory management can be annoying and Sheva's AI will occasionally watch you die, but stopping Albert Wesker’s plans for "Complete Global Saturation" through pure geological violence makes it all worth it. Buy it for the co-op, stay for the rock-punching finale. 10/10, Wesker and that boulder never stood a chance.
label: analysis
